subject: Gastroenterology
title: Diverticulosis
preambleMarkdown:
# Diverticulosis



> **Clinical-review notice:** This is a bullet-first examination and clinical-orientation draft. Local protocols, medication doses, procedural thresholds, pregnancy/pediatric pathways, emergency workflows, and jurisdiction-specific guidance must be checked before clinical use.

sections.heading: Overview
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Diverticulosis is a clinical condition or syndrome in gastroenterology.
- This article focuses on GI anatomy, motility, inflammation, bleeding, obstruction, liver-pancreas-biliary disease, and nutrition.
- The practical clinical priority is gastroenterology diagnosis, management, and prevention.
- Start by deciding whether the presentation is stable, time-sensitive, complicated, recurrent, or incidental.
- Imported source notes are preserved below for clinical review and citation reconciliation.

sections.heading: Key Points
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Identify unstable, time-sensitive, or function-threatening presentations first.
- Anchor the assessment of Diverticulosis to the patient context: age, pregnancy status when relevant, immune status, comorbid disease, medications, prior episodes, and local resources.
- Use focused history and examination before broad testing.
- Choose investigations that change diagnosis, severity assessment, treatment, disposition, or follow-up.
- Treat reversible danger early and give clear safety-net instructions.

sections.heading: Definition
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Diverticulosis is best defined by the specific clinical problem, anatomic site, mechanism, severity, and care setting.
- For this topic, the core concept is gastroenterology diagnosis, management, and prevention.
- Separate suspected, confirmed, recurrent, complicated, and resolved disease states.

sections.heading: Classification
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Acute, subacute, chronic, recurrent, or resolved state.
- Mild, moderate, severe, complicated, or life-threatening disease.
- Primary disease versus secondary complication or medication effect.
- Outpatient, emergency department, inpatient, ICU, pregnancy, pediatric, or older-adult setting.

sections.heading: Epidemiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- The epidemiology of Diverticulosis depends on age, sex, geography, exposure, comorbid disease, medication use, pregnancy status when relevant, and ascertainment setting.
- In gastroenterology, exam questions usually test the common population plus the high-risk exception.
- Risk estimates should be updated from local data or current guideline sources before publication.

sections.heading: Anatomy
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Identify the organ, tissue, vessel, nerve, compartment, joint, pathway, or system most directly involved in Diverticulosis.
- Map nearby structures that explain referred pain, spread, obstruction, ischemia, neurologic deficits, imaging findings, or procedure risk.
- For non-anatomic topics, focus on the structure most likely to change symptoms, testing, or treatment.

sections.heading: Physiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Define the normal function that Diverticulosis disrupts or depends on.
- Connect the physiology to symptoms, examination findings, laboratory abnormalities, imaging appearance, and treatment targets.
- Consider compensation, reserve, feedback loops, and decompensation triggers.

sections.heading: Pathophysiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- The central mechanism in Diverticulosis should explain why the patient develops the key symptoms and complications.
- Mechanisms may include obstruction, inflammation, ischemia, infection, immune injury, degeneration, trauma, thrombosis, endocrine imbalance, toxicity, neoplasia, or functional dysregulation.
- Distinguish the primary process from secondary complications and treatment-related harm.

sections.heading: Etiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider the common causes of Diverticulosis first, then actively check for dangerous but less common causes.
- Review congenital, acquired, infectious, inflammatory, traumatic, metabolic, neoplastic, medication-related, toxic, pregnancy-related, immune, and idiopathic causes when relevant.
- Use the time course and exposure history to narrow the cause.

sections.heading: Risk Factors
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Risk changes with age, family history, baseline disease, immune status, pregnancy, medications, procedures, travel, occupation, environment, and prior episodes.
- For Diverticulosis, risk factors should guide pretest probability and prevention, not replace objective assessment.
- Document modifiable risks that can be addressed during treatment or follow-up.

sections.heading: Genetics
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider inherited disease when presentation is early, familial, recurrent, syndromic, unusually severe, or associated with congenital findings.
- Ask about family history, ancestry when clinically relevant, consanguinity, pregnancy losses, and similar symptoms in relatives.
- Use genetic testing when results change diagnosis, screening, treatment, reproductive counseling, or family risk assessment.

sections.heading: Microbiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider infection when fever, exposure, immunocompromise, procedure, device, wound, systemic inflammation, or rapid clinical decline is present.
- Identify the likely source, route of spread, organism group, need for cultures, need for isolation, and need for source control.
- Antimicrobial decisions should account for severity, host risk, allergies, renal or hepatic function, pregnancy status, local resistance, and prior cultures.

sections.heading: Clinical Presentation
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Symptoms and signs depend on acuity, organ system involved, severity, comorbidities, medications, and prior episodes.
- Classic findings help recognition but absence of a classic finding does not exclude serious disease.
- Complications may be the first presentation in older adults, immunocompromised patients, and patients with limited follow-up.

sections.heading: History
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Ask onset, duration, progression, triggers, exposures, associated symptoms, prior episodes, and treatments already tried.
- Review medications, allergies, procedures, devices, travel, occupation, sexual history when relevant, pregnancy status when relevant, family history, and baseline function.
- For Diverticulosis, ask targeted questions that separate uncomplicated disease from dangerous complications.

sections.heading: Physical Examination
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Start with vital signs, mental status, perfusion, respiratory effort, hydration, pain severity, and overall appearance.
- Perform a focused gastroenterology examination that directly tests the suspected mechanism.
- Look for systemic involvement, complications, mimics, and findings that change urgency or disposition.

sections.heading: Red Flags
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Shock, altered mental status, respiratory distress, severe hypoxemia, syncope, seizure, major bleeding, or rapidly progressive symptoms.
- Severe pain out of proportion, new neurologic deficit, threatened airway, sepsis, peritonitis, limb ischemia, compartment syndrome, or vision threat.
- Any Diverticulosis presentation with organ dysfunction, pregnancy risk, immunocompromise, failed initial treatment, or diagnostic uncertainty needs a lower threshold for escalation.

sections.heading: Differential Diagnosis
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Compare Diverticulosis with disorders that share the same dominant symptom, anatomic site, exposure, laboratory pattern, or imaging abnormality.
- Prioritize dangerous mimics that would be harmed by delay or by the wrong treatment.
- Use acuity, location, risk factors, examination findings, labs, imaging, and response to treatment to narrow the differential.

sections.heading: Diagnostic Approach
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- First decide whether Diverticulosis is unstable, complicated, recurrent, incidental, or safe for outpatient workup.
- Use the highest-yield initial test for the suspected syndrome rather than ordering broad panels without a decision point.
- If early tests are negative but suspicion remains high, reassess the history, repeat focused examination, and choose second-line testing based on the remaining differential.

sections.heading: Investigations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Use labs to assess severity, complications, treatment safety, and alternative diagnoses.
- Use imaging, bedside tests, cultures, tissue sampling, functional testing, or procedures when results change management.
- Interpret results with pretest probability and clinical context.
- Avoid tests that add risk, delay, or incidental findings without changing the next step.

sections.heading: Diagnostic Criteria
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Diagnosis of Diverticulosis should combine compatible clinical pattern with accepted laboratory, imaging, procedural, histologic, physiologic, or scoring criteria when available.
- Do not diagnose from one nonspecific abnormality when the full syndrome does not fit.
- Document uncertainty and the follow-up plan when the diagnosis remains provisional.

sections.heading: Severity Classification
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Severity depends on stability, organ dysfunction, symptom burden, anatomic extent, complications, treatment risk, and ability to follow up.
- Identify who needs urgent referral, admission, ICU care, procedure, surgery, isolation, rehabilitation, or close outpatient review.
- Use validated disease-specific scoring or staging systems when they exist and are available locally.

sections.heading: Management
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Assess stability first, then treat the underlying cause and the complications already present.
- Use supportive care, medications, procedures, rehabilitation, and prevention according to severity and contraindications.
- Escalate to specialty care when diagnosis is uncertain, severe disease is present, or treatment risk is high.

### Initial Management

- Confirm whether Diverticulosis is stable, unstable, incidental, recurrent, or complicated.
- Assess airway, breathing, circulation, mental status, pain, hydration, perfusion, and immediate threats.
- Escalate early when red flags, organ dysfunction, procedural need, or diagnostic uncertainty is present.

### Non-pharmacological Management

- Use education, observation, rehabilitation, isolation, procedural planning, or environmental change when it addresses gastroenterology diagnosis, management, and prevention.
- Tailor activity, diet, wound, exposure, work, driving, return-to-play, or safety advice to the clinical setting.

### Pharmacological Management

- Use medication only when the indication is clear and contraindications have been checked.
- Verify dose, route, duration, interactions, allergies, renal and hepatic adjustment, pregnancy or lactation status, and monitoring.

### Procedures

- Use procedures when they confirm diagnosis, obtain tissue or fluid, provide source control, relieve obstruction, restore alignment, repair anatomy, or treat complications.
- Check consent, anatomy, imaging, bleeding risk, infection risk, sedation risk, equipment, and aftercare before proceeding.

### Surgical Management

- Surgical or procedural referral is needed for instability, failed medical management, obstruction, perforation, ischemia, uncontrolled bleeding, malignancy concern, repairable structural disease, or unsafe bedside management.
- Timing depends on severity, anatomy, operative risk, patient goals, and local expertise.

sections.heading: Monitoring
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Track symptoms, vital signs, function, pain, intake/output when relevant, focused examination findings, key labs, adverse effects, and response to therapy.
- For Diverticulosis, monitoring should detect deterioration, treatment toxicity, recurrence, and missed complications.
- Escalate if expected improvement does not occur or new red flags appear.

sections.heading: Follow-up
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Confirm diagnosis, severity, treatment response, adherence, side effects, and patient understanding.
- Arrange repeat examination, labs, imaging, rehabilitation, surveillance, medication review, or specialty care when indicated.
- Give a clear reassessment interval and explicit return precautions.

sections.heading: Complications
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Complications of Diverticulosis may be acute, chronic, recurrent, treatment-related, functional, psychosocial, or procedure-related.
- Prioritize complications that threaten life, airway, limb, vision, fertility, neurologic function, organ function, or public health.
- Document warning symptoms that require urgent reassessment.

sections.heading: Prognosis
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Prognosis depends on cause, severity, timing of recognition, comorbid disease, age, treatment response, complications, and follow-up reliability.
- Early recognition of dangerous Diverticulosis patterns improves disposition and reduces preventable complications.
- Published prognostic statistics should be checked against current specialty sources before release.

sections.heading: Prevention
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Address modifiable risk factors, medication safety, vaccination or prophylaxis when relevant, exposure reduction, screening, and recurrence prevention.
- For Diverticulosis, prevention should target the cause, the complication pathway, and the follow-up failure points.
- Use patient-specific counseling rather than generic reassurance.

sections.heading: Patient Education
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Explain Diverticulosis in plain language, including what is known, what remains uncertain, and what symptoms should trigger urgent care.
- Review medication purpose, expected benefit, side effects, duration, and what to do if symptoms worsen.
- Confirm the patient understands follow-up, restrictions, wound or device care when relevant, and return precautions.

sections.heading: Nursing Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Monitor vital signs, pain, function, mental status, intake/output when relevant, wounds or devices, and response to treatment.
- Escalate fever, bleeding, respiratory change, neurologic change, perfusion change, uncontrolled pain, medication reaction, or worsening function.
- Reinforce education, discharge instructions, safe mobility, adherence, and follow-up logistics.

sections.heading: Pharmacy Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Check allergies, renal and hepatic function, pregnancy or lactation status, interactions, duplication, contraindications, adherence barriers, and monitoring needs.
- For Diverticulosis, medication review should include both treatment drugs and drugs that may cause, worsen, or mimic the problem.
- High-risk drugs require a documented indication, duration, monitoring plan, and counseling.

sections.heading: Dentistry Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Ask about bleeding risk, anticoagulants, antiplatelets, immunosuppression, prosthetic material, active infection, and relevant medication adverse effects before invasive dental work.
- Coordinate with the treating clinician when medication interruption, prophylaxis, or infection control is being considered.
- Diverticulosis does not automatically imply dental antibiotic prophylaxis; use condition-specific indications and local guidance.

sections.heading: Special Populations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Pediatrics: use age-specific normal values, developmental context, weight-based dosing, caregiver education, and safeguarding awareness.
- Pregnancy: stabilize the mother first, choose pregnancy-aware testing and medications when possible, and involve obstetrics for significant disease.
- Older adults: consider frailty, atypical presentation, falls, delirium, kidney function, polypharmacy, cognition, and goals of care.
- Renal or hepatic impairment: adjust diagnostic contrast, medications, dosing, and monitoring.
- Immunocompromised patients: lower the threshold for atypical infection, severe disease, cultures, imaging, admission, and interaction review.

sections.heading: Guideline Comparison
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Current guidance for Diverticulosis may vary by country, specialty society, age group, pregnancy status, disease severity, and available resources.
- Compare local policy with major society guidance for diagnostic thresholds, treatment sequencing, drug choice, procedural timing, and follow-up intervals.
- Document where local practice differs from international guidance and why.

sections.heading: Clinical Pearls
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Stabilize first when the patient is unstable; diagnostic precision comes after immediate threats are controlled.
- Match each test to a decision it will change.
- Reassess after treatment because nonresponse is often a diagnostic clue.
- For Diverticulosis, the highest-yield clinical move is to identify severity and the next safe action.

sections.heading: Common Mistakes
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Treating Diverticulosis as a label instead of confirming mechanism, severity, and complications.
- Ordering broad tests without a focused question.
- Missing red flags because the initial symptom sounds common.
- Ignoring pregnancy status, renal function, hepatic function, allergies, interactions, or follow-up barriers.
- Discharging without clear return precautions.

sections.heading: Exam Pearls
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Step 1: know the mechanism, anatomy, microbiology, pharmacology, or physiology behind the topic.
- Step 2 CK: know the next best diagnostic or management step.
- Step 3: know disposition, monitoring, complications, follow-up, and patient safety.
- Board exams: know contraindications, high-risk exceptions, and when to escalate.

sections.heading: High-Yield Facts
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Know the classic presentation, first diagnostic step, first treatment step, dangerous complication, and follow-up need.
- Severity and stability decide disposition.
- Treatment should include prevention of recurrence when possible.

sections.heading: Evidence Gaps
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- This Diverticulosis draft still needs specialty review before publication.
- Medication doses, procedural thresholds, screening intervals, risk scores, and guideline claims should be localized.
- Imported notes, when present, should be reconciled with current evidence and cited sources.

sections.heading: References
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Current specialty society guidance, local protocols, and institutional pathways should be checked during clinical review.
- When imported source notes are present, reconcile them against current references before publication.

### Source Excerpt: Diverticulosis

_Source lines: 10360-10764._

```text
Diverticulosis
A. General Characteristics
1. Caused by increased intraluminal pressure-inner layer of colon
bulges through the focal area of weakness in the colon wall (usually
an area of blood vessel penetration)
2. Risk factors
a. Low-fiber, high-fat diets: Constipation causes intraluminal
pressures to increase.

b. Physical inactivity
c. Obesity

3. Prevalence increases with age
4. The most common location is the sigmoid colon. However,
diverticula may occur anywhere in the colon
Quick HIT
Diverticulosis (pouches in the colon wall) should be distinguished
from diverticulitis (inflammation or infection of diverticula).

Diverticulitis is a complication of diverticulosis.

B. Clinical Features
1. Usually asymptomatic and discovered incidentally on imaging (e.g.,
CT abdomen/pelvis) or when a complication occurs (diverticular
bleed or diverticulitis)
2. Vague left lower quadrant (LLQ) discomfort, bloating, constipation/
diarrhea may be present
3. Only 10% to 20% become symptomatic (i.e., develop complications
-see below)
C. Diagnosis
1. Barium enema or CT abdomen/pelvis (often incidentally found)
2. Abdominal x-rays are usually normal and are not diagnostic for
diverticulosis
Quick HIT
- Complications of diverticulosis include painless rectal bleeding
and diverticulitis.

- Complications of diverticulitis include bowel obstruction,
abscess, and fistulas.

D. Treatment
1. High-fiber diet to increase stool bulk and reduce constipation
2. Dietary fiber supplements (such as psyllium)
E. Complications
1. Painless rectal bleeding (up to 40% of patients)
a. Bleeding usually stops spontaneously. No further treatment is
necessary in these patients.

b. Bleeding can be severe in about 5% of patients. Colonoscopy
may be performed to locate and treat the site of bleeding
(tagged RBC scan in certain cases if colonoscopy does not

localize the site). If bleeding is persistent and/or recurrent,
surgery (segmental colectomy) may be needed.

CLINICAL PEARL 3-1
Complications of Diverticulitis
- Abscess formation (can be drained either percutaneously under CT
guidance or surgically)
- Colovesical fistula-accounts for 50% of fistulas secondary to
diverticulitis; 50% close spontaneously
- Obstruction-due to chronic inflammation and thickening of bowel wall
- Free colonic perforation-uncommon but catastrophic (leads to
peritonitis)
1. Diverticulitis (15% to 25% of patients)
a. Occurs when feces become impacted in the diverticulum,
leading to erosion and microperforation.

b. Can be complicated (see also Clinical Pearl 3-1) or
uncomplicated. Uncomplicated diverticulitis accounts for most
cases and refers to diverticulitis without the complications listed
in Clinical Pearl 3-1.

c. Clinical features: fever, LLQ pain, leukocytosis.

- Other possible features: alteration in BOWEL habits
(constipation or diarrhea), vomiting, and sometimes a
painful mass on rectal examination if inflammation is near
the rectum.

e. Diagnostic tests
- CT scan (abdomen and pelvis) with oral and IV contrast
is the test of choice; it may reveal a swollen, edematous
bowel wall or an abscess.

- Abdominal radiographs help in excluding other potential
causes of LLQ pain, and can rule out ileus or obstruction
(indicated by air-fluid levels, distention), and perforation
(indicated by free air).

- Avoid barium enema and colonoscopy during acute
diverticulitis due to the risk of perforation.

i. Treatment
- Uncomplicated diverticulitis: managed with antibiotics,
bowel rest (NPO), IV fluids, and pain control. Mild episodes
can be treated on outpatient basis with oral antibiotics if
patient has reliable follow-up and has few or no comorbid

conditions. If symptoms persist after 3 to 4 days, surgical
resection of the involved segment may be necessary.

Antibiotics are generally continued for 7 to 10 days. After
successful treatment, about one-third has recurrence.

Surgery recommended for recurrent episodes.

- Complicated diverticulitis: IV antibiotics, bowel rest (NPO),
IV fluids. Surgery is often indicated for complications
(perforation, large abscess, fistula). Antibiotics are usually
continued for 10 to 14 days, but may be shorter if definitive
source control is obtained (e.g., abscess drainage).

Quick HIT
- Diverticulitis recurs in about 30% of patients treated medically,
usually within the first 5 years.

Quick HIT
- Diverticulosis-barium enema or CT scan are the tests of
choice.

- Diverticulitis-CT scan is the test of choice (avoid barium
enema and colonoscopy).

Quick HIT
A small percentage of patients with bleeding arteriovenous
malformations have aortic stenosis (Heyde syndrome). However, no
cause-and-effect relationship has been proven.

Quick HIT
- Acute mesenteric ischemia is sudden interruption of blood
supply to the intestine. Chronic mesenteric ischemia is usually
from chronic atherosclerotic disease of the splanchnic arteries.

- Patients with acute mesenteric ischemia often have pre-existing
heart disease (e.g., congestive heart failure, CAD).

Angiodysplasia of the Colon (Arteriovenous
Malformations, Vascular Ectasia)
- Tortuous, dilated veins in submucosa of the colon (usually proximal)

wall.

- A common cause of lower GI bleeding in patients over age 60.

- Bleeding is usually low grade, but 15% of patients may have massive
hemorrhage if veins rupture.

- Diagnosed by colonoscopy (preferred over angiography).

- In about 90% of patients, bleeding stops spontaneously.

- It can frequently be treated by colonoscopic hemostasis of the lesion.

If bleeding persists, a right hemicolectomy should be considered.

Acute Mesenteric Ischemia
A. Introduction
1. Results from a compromised blood supply, usually to the superior
mesenteric vessels.

2. There are four types (three are due to arterial disease, one due to
venous disease):
a. Arterial embolism (50% of cases): Almost all emboli are of a
cardiac origin (e.g., atrial fibrillation, MI, valvular disease).

b. Arterial thrombosis (25% of cases).

- Most of these patients have atherosclerotic disease (e.g.,
coronary artery disease [CAD], PAD, stroke) at other sites.

- Acute occlusion occurs over pre-existing atherosclerotic
disease. The acute event may be due to a decrease in cardiac
output (e.g., resulting from MI, CHF) or plaque rupture.

- Collateral circulation has usually developed.

f. Nonocclusive mesenteric ischemia (20% of cases).

- Splanchnic vasoconstriction secondary to low cardiac
output.

- Typically seen in critically ill elderly patients.

i. Venous thrombosis (<10% of cases). Many predisposing
factors-infection, hypercoagulable states, oral contraceptives,
portal HTN, malignancy, pancreatitis.

3. The overall mortality rate for all types of acute mesenteric ischemia
is about 60% to 70%. If bowel infarction has occurred, the
mortality rate can exceed 90%.

Quick HIT
Differences in Presentation of Types of Acute Mesenteric
Ischemia:
- Embolic-symptoms are more sudden and painful than other
causes
- Arterial thrombosis-symptoms are more gradual and less

severe than embolic causes
- Nonocclusive ischemia-typically occurs in critically ill patients
- Venous thrombosis-symptoms may be present for several
days or even weeks, with gradual worsening
B. Clinical Features
1. Classic presentation is acute onset of severe abdominal pain out
of proportion to physical examination findings. Pain is due to
ischemia and possibly infarction of intestines, analogous to MI in
CAD.

a. The abdominal examination may appear benign even when
there is severe ischemia. This can lead to a delay in diagnosis.

b. The acuteness and the severity of pain vary depending on the
type of acute mesenteric ischemia (see Quick Hit).

2. Anorexia, vomiting.

3. GI bleeding.

4. Peritonitis, sepsis, and shock may be present in advanced disease.

Quick HIT
- Signs of intestinal infarction include hypotension, tachypnea,
lactic acidosis, fever, and altered mental status (eventually
leading to shock).

- Check the lactate level if acute mesenteric ischemia is
suspected.

C. Diagnosis
1. High clinical suspicion in patients with known risk factors (CAD,
Afib, recent MI, etc.).

2. CT angiography of abdomen/pelvis is the test of choice. Can also
evaluate for other etiologies of abdominal pain.

3. Mesenteric angiography is the definitive test of choice and is done
if CT angiography is equivocal.

4. "Thumbprinting" can be seen on barium enema due to thickened
edematous mucosal folds.

D. Treatment
1. Supportive measures: IV fluids, broad-spectrum antibiotics, and
pain management.

2. Avoid vasopressors, which may exacerbate ischemia.

3. Anticoagulation with unfractionated heparin.

4. Surgery (resection of nonviable bowel or embolectomy) may be

needed if signs of peritonitis develop.

5. Catheter-directed interventions can be done if hemodynamically
stable, no peritoneal signs, and low suspicion for advanced bowel
ischemia. These include catheter-directed pharmacologic and
mechanical thrombolysis, angioplasty, and stenting.

6. Direct intraarterial infusion of papaverine (vasodilator) into the
superior mesenteric system during arteriography is an option for
nonocclusive mesenteric ischemia.

Chronic Mesenteric Ischemia
- Caused by atherosclerotic occlusive disease of main mesenteric
vessels (celiac artery, superior and inferior mesenteric arteries).

- Abdominal angina-dull pain, typically postprandial (when there is
increased demand for splanchnic blood flow); analogous to anginal
pain of CAD.

- Significant weight loss may occur due to abdominal angina.

- CT abdomen with IV contrast is the initial test of choice.

Mesenteric arteriography confirms the diagnosis if CT is equivocal,
and can be used for therapeutic intervention.

- Options for definitive treatment include surgical revascularization or
mesenteric angioplasty and stenting. Revascularization leads to pain
relief in 90% of cases.

Quick HIT
Patients with acute colonic pseudo-obstruction are usually ill and
commonly have a history of recent surgery or medical illness.

Acute Colonic Pseudo-Obstruction (Ogilvie
Syndrome)
- An unusual problem in which signs, symptoms, and radiographic
evidence of large bowel obstruction are present, but there is no
mechanical obstruction.

- Common causes include recent surgery or trauma, electrolyte serious
medical illnesses (e.g., sepsis, malignancy, dementia, MI, metabolic
derangements), and medications (e.g., opioids, psychotropic drugs,
anticholinergics).

- The diagnosis cannot be confirmed until mechanical obstruction of the
colon is excluded.

- Treatment consists of stopping any offending agent and supportive
measures (IV fluids, electrolyte repletion, NG tube decompression,
serial abdominal examinations).

- Neostigmine can be given if supportive measures are not effective.

Decompression with gentle enemas or nasogastric suction may be
helpful. Colonoscopic decompression is usually successful if the above
measures fail.

- Surgical decompression with cecostomy or colostomy is a last resort.

Quick HIT
Whenever there is colonic distention and the colon diameter
exceeds 10 cm, bowel is at risk of impending rupture leading to
peritonitis and even death; decompress immediately.

Colonic Volvulus
A. General Characteristics
1. Defined as twisting of a loop of intestine about its mesenteric
attachment site.

2. May result in obstruction or vascular compromise with the
potential for necrosis and/or perforation if untreated.

3. Worldwide, the most common site is the sigmoid colon (75% of
all cases).

4. Cecal volvulus accounts for 25% of all cases.

5. Risk factors
a. Chronic illness, age, institutionalization, and CNS disease
increase risk of sigmoid volvulus.

b. Cecal volvulus is due to congenital lack of fixation of the right
colon and tends to occur in younger patients.

c. Chronic constipation, laxative abuse, antimotility drugs.

d. Prior abdominal surgery.

B. Clinical Features
1. Acute to subacute onset of colicky abdominal pain
2. Obstipation, abdominal distention and tenderness
3. Anorexia, nausea, vomiting
C. Diagnosis
1. Plain abdominal films-identifies sigmoid volvulus in 60% of cases.

a. Sigmoid volvulus-classic finding is Omega loop sign (or bent
inner-tube shape), indicates a dilated sigmoid colon.

b. Cecal volvulus (distention of cecum and small bowel)-classic
finding is coffee bean sign, indicates a large air-fluid level in
RLQ.

2. CT of abdomen-typical pattern is "whirl sign" caused by dilated
colon twisting around mesenteric vessels (see Figure 3-1). May also
show absence of rectal gas. Can identify complications
(pneumatosis intestinalis, portal venous gas).

3. Sigmoidoscopy-preferred diagnostic and therapeutic test for
sigmoid volvulus (not for cecal volvulus); leads to successful
treatment (untwisting and decompression) in many cases.

4. Barium enema-reveals the narrowing of the colon at the point
where it is twisted ("bird's beak").

Quick HIT
Do not perform a barium enema if perforation is suspected!

D. Treatment
1. Sigmoid volvulus: Nonoperative reduction (decompression via
sigmoidoscopy) is successful in >75% of cases. The recurrence rate
is high, so elective sigmoid colon resection is recommended.

2. Cecal volvulus: Emergent surgery is indicated.

FIGURE
3-1 Axial contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen demonstrating sigmoid
volvulus with the whirl sign (arrow) at site of torsion. On right, coronal CT
showing dilated sigmoid colonic loop in the left upper quadrant, with two
walls opposed together (arrowhead).

(Reprinted with permission from Singh AK. Gastrointestinal Imaging: The Essentials. Wolters
Kluwer; 2016. Figure 5A.22.)

Diseases of the Liver
```
